content.text: On-Call Rotation Principles for Claude Services

An on-call rotation for Claude API integration services has three goals: ensure that every alert gets an expert response within SLO-defined windows, distribute the on-call burden equitably so engineers are not chronically exhausted, and build a rotation that scales as the service and team grow. These goals are in tension — expert response favors a small rotation of specialists, while equitable distribution favors a large rotation, and scaling favors a structured process.

The correct design resolves this tension with a tiered rotation: a primary SRE tier that carries the 24/7 pager, a secondary feature engineer tier that is escalated to for domain-specific incidents, and a manager tier that serves as a fallback and war room opener. Each tier has a distinct role, a distinct escalation condition, and a distinct rotation schedule.

The primary goal of rotation design is to prevent any single engineer from being the single point of knowledge and the single point of exhaustion. A rotation where one person can always 'fix it faster' is not a rotation — it is a hidden dependency.

attrs.alt: On-call rotation structure — primary SRE tier, secondary feature engineer tier, and persistent manager tier

content.text: The primary SRE carries the 24/7 pager for a fixed rotation period (typically 7 days). They are the first responder for all alerts — P0, P1, and P2. Their responsibility is to acknowledge, triage, and either resolve or escalate within the SLA window. They do not need to be the subject matter expert for every incident; their job is to drive the response, not to debug every issue personally.
5 minutes during hypercare, 10 minutes steady state
Cannot resolve within 15 minutes → escalate to Tier 2

The secondary tier consists of feature engineers from the team that owns the Claude integration. They are on call for escalation only — they do not carry a 24/7 pager, but they are expected to respond to a direct page within 15 minutes. Their role is to provide domain knowledge when the primary SRE has identified that the incident requires deep understanding of the application logic or model behavior.
Escalation only — paged by Tier 1, not directly by alerting system
Tier 1 cannot diagnose after 10 minutes → escalate to Tier 2
0–2 (healthy); >5 indicates alerts not being triaged before escalation

The manager tier is a persistent assignment — the SRE manager or engineering manager is always the third escalation target. They are paged only when both Tier 1 and Tier 2 are unreachable after 10 minutes. Their role is not technical diagnosis; it is to open a war room, contact the right people, and ensure the incident has a functioning response before returning to oversight.
The manager tier does not rotate because the role requires organizational authority, not technical depth.
If the manager is on vacation, a named deputy is designated for that period.
The manager is never paged for P2 incidents — P2 alerts go to Tier 1 only.

The minimum viable on-call rotation is 3 engineers — each engineer is on call once every 3 weeks. This is the absolute minimum for preventing immediate burnout. The recommended size is 5–8 engineers for most Claude API integration teams, balancing the cost of knowledge distribution (more engineers to train) against the benefit of reduced on-call frequency.
High for noisy services — minimum viable
Very low — but harder to maintain knowledge

Rotation size is bounded by knowledge distribution: every engineer on the rotation must be able to respond to any alert with at least basic triage capability. Growing the rotation beyond 8–10 requires runbook quality to compensate for reduced individual expertise per engineer. If runbooks are incomplete or untested, increasing rotation size reduces response quality.
Grow the rotation by improving runbook quality, not by adding engineers before the runbooks are ready. A 10-person rotation with bad runbooks is worse than a 5-person rotation with good ones.

PagerDuty is the most common on-call scheduling tool. A complete Claude services rotation configuration requires three schedules (Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3) and three escalation policies (P0, P1, P2) that route to them in order.

# pagerduty_rotation_config.py — configure on-call schedules via PagerDuty API
import requests

PD_TOKEN = "your-pagerduty-api-token"
HEADERS = {
    "Authorization": f"Token token={PD_TOKEN}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
}

def create_weekly_rotation_schedule(
    name: str,
    timezone: str,
    users: list[str],           # list of PagerDuty user IDs
    start_date: str,            # ISO 8601 date string for rotation start
    rotation_period_days: int = 7,
) -> dict:
    """Create a 7-day on-call rotation schedule."""
    payload = {
        "schedule": {
            "type": "schedule",
            "name": name,
            "time_zone": timezone,
            "schedule_layers": [
                {
                    "name": "Primary Rotation",
                    "start": f"{start_date}T18:00:00",  # Handoff at 18:00 local
                    "rotation_virtual_start": f"{start_date}T18:00:00",
                    "rotation_turn_length_seconds": rotation_period_days * 86400,
                    "users": [{"user": {"id": uid, "type": "user"}} for uid in users],
                }
            ],
        }
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        "https://api.pagerduty.com/schedules",
        headers=HEADERS,
        json=payload,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["schedule"]


# Create primary SRE rotation
sre_schedule = create_weekly_rotation_schedule(
    name="Claude Services - Primary SRE",
    timezone="America/Los_Angeles",
    users=["P1ALICE", "P1BOB", "P1CAROL", "P1DAVE", "P1EVE"],
    start_date="2024-04-21",
)

# Create secondary feature engineer rotation (same structure)
feature_schedule = create_weekly_rotation_schedule(
    name="Claude Services - Feature Eng Secondary",
    timezone="America/Los_Angeles",
    users=["P2CHARLIE", "P2DANA", "P2EVA"],
    start_date="2024-04-21",
)

print(f"SRE schedule ID: {sre_schedule['id']}")
print(f"Feature eng schedule ID: {feature_schedule['id']}")

A shift handoff is a 15-minute synchronous meeting between the outgoing and incoming on-call engineer at the start of each rotation period. Handoffs prevent the most common cause of missed responses in the first hours of a new shift: the incoming engineer is unaware of ongoing investigations, degraded dependencies, or alert suppression decisions made by their predecessor.
Any open or monitoring-phase incidents; current status and who is still watching
Any alerts currently silenced or reduced in severity; reason and expiry time
Any deploys or config changes in the last 24 hours that might explain alert behavior
Any upstream dependencies (including Anthropic API itself) that are degraded
Any runbooks that the outgoing engineer found inaccurate and flagged for update
Anything unusual that did not trigger an alert but that the incoming engineer should know

# On-Call Handoff Notes — Claude Integration Service
## Outgoing: Alice (Week of Apr 14)
## Incoming: Bob (Week of Apr 21)
## Handoff time: 2024-04-21 18:00 PDT

### Ongoing incidents
- INC-20240419-002: P2 intermittent 503s from model-serving tier, ~0.2% of requests.
  Status: Monitoring. No action required unless rate increases above 0.5%.
  Watch the `model_server_5xx_rate` panel in Grafana.

### Alert suppressions
- `QueueDepthHigh` alert silenced until 2024-04-22 08:00 PDT.
  Reason: planned batch job running overnight; queue depth will be elevated by design.
  If job is not done by 08:00, re-evaluate.

### Recent changes
- Apr 20 14:30: tokenizer config updated (PR #2187). No issues seen in 28h observation window.
- Apr 18 09:00: canary bumped from 5% to 15%. Error rate normal.

### Known degraded dependencies
- None at handoff time.

### Runbook updates needed
- Runbook #14 (rollback-tokenizer-service): step 3 has an outdated IAM role name.
  Correct role is `sre-prod-model-rollback-v2`. Filed as runbook-update-007.

### Oddities to watch
- Haiku model p99 latency is ~15% above baseline but within SLO. Trending for 3 days.
  Not enough to alert, but if it continues or worsens, open an investigation.

A global team that spans multiple time zones can implement a follow-the-sun rotation, where different teams cover different time windows, eliminating after-hours on-call shifts. This is the most humane model for globally distributed teams but requires precise handoff procedures and careful overlap management.
00:00–10:00 UTC (8 AM–6 PM IST / 9 AM–7 PM JST)

The 2-hour overlap windows are critical — each handoff should happen during the overlap, not at the hard boundary. An engineer handing off at 01:00 UTC to an APAC colleague who starts at 00:00 UTC has a 3-hour overlap; use at least 30 minutes of it for the handoff call.

Follow-the-sun only works if the handoff discipline is perfect. A missed handoff at 01:00 UTC means the incoming APAC engineer has no context for any ongoing issue — and they have no one to call at that hour.

On-call health is as important as service reliability. An on-call rotation that pages engineers 20 times per week produces exhausted, demoralized engineers who miss alerts because they have trained themselves to ignore them. Monitoring on-call health prevents the rotation from becoming unsustainable.
Alert triage review: are all pages actionable?
Reduce after-hours alert sensitivity or improve daytime resolution
Time spent on-call incidents per week (hours)
Identify top 3 alert classes and reduce them
Percentage of pages that required action
Review and tune low-action alert classes
Review alert routing and rotation health

On-call health metrics should be reviewed in the monthly cross-functional review alongside error budget data. A rotation with acceptable error budget numbers but poor on-call health is not sustainable — the team will degrade before the metrics show it.

On-call rotation design for Claude services uses a three-tier model: a primary SRE tier carrying the 24/7 pager on a weekly rotation, a secondary feature engineer tier escalated to for domain knowledge, and a persistent manager tier as the final escalation. The minimum viable rotation is 3 engineers (1 week in 3); the recommended target is 5–8 engineers (1 week in 5–8). Rotation size is bounded by runbook quality — adding engineers without improving runbooks reduces response quality. PagerDuty schedules configure the rotation periods and escalation policies enforce the three-tier structure with timeout-based escalation (5 min for Tier 1 ACK, 10 min for Tier 2 escalation). Shift handoffs are 15-minute synchronous calls covering ongoing incidents, alert suppressions, recent changes, and degraded dependencies. On-call health metrics (pages per week, after-hours pages, time spent, actionable page rate) must be tracked alongside SLO metrics — a rotation that is reliable but exhausting is not sustainable.
Three tiers: SRE primary (24/7 pager), feature eng secondary (escalation only), manager (fallback + war room opener).
Minimum rotation: 3 engineers. Recommended: 5–8. Size bounded by runbook quality, not headcount.
PagerDuty escalation: Tier 1 paged first; 5 min no ACK → Tier 2; 10 min no ACK → Tier 3 manager.
Shift handoff: 15-min call at rotation start covering incidents, suppressions, recent changes, degraded dependencies.
Follow-the-sun: requires 2-hour overlap windows and perfect handoff discipline — missed handoffs cause context gaps.
On-call health metrics: pages/week ≤ 5, after-hours ≤ 2, actionable page rate > 80%. Review monthly.
